The architecture behind What I Did Today: Next.js, IndexedDB, Supabase, Web Crypto, encrypted sharing, and AI summaries.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I built What I Did Today to solve a problem I kept encountering: daily work is easy to do and surprisingly hard to remember.
By the time a standup, one-on-one, status report, or performance review arrives, the details are scattered across chat, email, calendars, tickets, and source control. I wanted a tool where I could quickly record the work while it was fresh, retrieve it later, and generate useful summaries.
The first version was a conventional authenticated web app backed by Supabase. As the product evolved, I reconsidered a fundamental assumption: should a private work journal require a server round trip — and should the database ever contain readable notes?
That led to a local-first architecture with optional encrypted synchronization.
This article explains the decisions, implementation, and trade-offs behind it.
The product requirements
The core experience had to remain simple:

• Open the app and start writing immediately.

• Organize notes automatically by year, month, week, and day.

• Support normal notes and action items.

• Search past work quickly.

• Generate AI summaries for a day or longer period.

• Share selected updates without exposing the entire journal.

• Work without requiring an account.
The security requirements added a second layer:

• Plaintext notes should be stored locally first.

• Cloud synchronization should be optional.

• Notes should be encrypted before leaving the browser.

• The server should not receive the vault key.

• Shared notes should use a separate key.

• Users should be able to export their data.
These requirements shaped every part of the stack.
The application stack
The app is built with:

• Next.js 16 using the App Router

• React 19 and TypeScript

• Tailwind CSS 4

• IndexedDB for local persistence

• Supabase Auth, PostgreSQL, and Row Level Security

• The browser Web Crypto API

• Resend for outbound email

• Playwright for end-to-end tests

• Vercel for deployment and performance monitoring
Next.js handles the UI, routing, API endpoints, and server-side integrations. Supabase provides authentication and a durable cloud database. IndexedDB is the working data store in the browser.
The important point is that Supabase is not the primary editor database anymore. The browser is.
Moving from cloud-first to local-first
In a typical cloud-first notes app, each edit eventually becomes an API request. The server database is the source of truth and the client keeps temporary state.
I reversed that relationship.
When a user edits a note, the app:

• Updates React state for immediate feedback.

• Saves the normalized note to IndexedDB.

• Records local history.

• Marks the note as pending synchronization.

• If the user is signed in and the vault is unlocked, encrypts and uploads it.
This creates a fast writing experience and allows guests to use the app without authentication.
The local database has stores for notes, history, metadata, pending synchronization, keys, and summaries. Notes include an owner namespace, synchronization status, local revision, and pending-sync flag.
Using an owner namespace matters because a browser can contain both guest notes and notes associated with an authenticated account.
The guest-to-account merge problem
Local-first applications have edge cases that cloud-first applications often avoid.
Consider this flow:

• A visitor writes a note as a guest.

• The note is saved under the guest namespace.

• The visitor signs in.

• The app switches to the account namespace.

• The encryption vault is still locked.
An early implementation displayed a new empty account note at step four. The guest note was still in IndexedDB, but it appeared to have disappeared.
The fix was to make guest-to-account reconciliation explicit.
When a person signs in with a locked vault, the account’s local note is seeded from the meaningful guest draft. It remains marked as pending. After the vault is unlocked, synchronization reconciles notes by both ID and date:

• A new date is uploaded as an encrypted note.

• A local note matching an existing cloud date is merged block by block.

• Duplicate text is removed.

• Empty duplicate records are deleted.

• The resulting cloud note is written back to the account namespace.
This is a useful local-first lesson: authentication changes identity, but it must not silently change ownership of unsynchronized data.
Encrypting notes in the browser
The encryption layer uses the Web Crypto API and AES-GCM with a 256-bit key.
AES-GCM provides authenticated encryption. In addition to hiding the plaintext, it detects ciphertext tampering. Every encryption operation uses a fresh random initialization vector.
The encrypted payload contains:
{
"v": 1,
"alg": "AES-GCM",
"iv": "base64url-encoded-random-iv",
"ciphertext": "base64url-encoded-ciphertext"
}The cloud row retains operational metadata such as the note ID, user ID, and date, but its readable content fields are empty. The actual note body and title live inside content_enc.
This is not metadata-private storage: the server can still know that a note exists and when it belongs in the timeline. The goal is content confidentiality, not hiding every access pattern.
Building the vault
Each account gets a random AES master key. Notes are encrypted with this key.
To support access from another device, the master key itself must be stored somewhere — but it cannot be stored as plaintext. The app therefore derives a wrapping key from the user’s vault passphrase using PBKDF2 with SHA-256, a random 16-byte salt, and 310,000 iterations.
The flow is:

• Generate a random 256-bit master key.

• Derive a wrapping key from the passphrase.

• Encrypt the raw master key with the wrapping key.

• Store only the salt and wrapped master key in the user profile.

• Unlock on another device by deriving the same wrapping key and decrypting the master key locally.
The raw master key also becomes the recovery key. It is shown once during vault creation and should be stored in a password manager.
The server cannot recover the vault from a normal password reset because it does not know the vault passphrase or recovery key.
That is a real trade-off. End-to-end encryption reduces the service provider’s power, including its power to rescue a user who loses every key.
Encrypted share links
Sharing creates another problem: a recipient needs to decrypt a note, but giving them the account’s master key would expose every note.
The solution is a unique random key for each share.
When creating a share, the browser:

• Generates a random share key.

• Encrypts the selected note with that key.

• Uploads the share ciphertext.

• Builds a URL such as:
https://whatididtoday.xyz/shared/<share-id>#key=<share-key>The key is after the #, in the URL fragment. Browsers do not include the fragment in the HTTP request, so the server receives the share ID but not the decryption key.
The shared page fetches the ciphertext and decrypts it in the recipient’s browser.
This follows the same practical pattern used by tools such as Excalidraw for encrypted links. Anyone who possesses the complete URL can read the shared content, so the link itself must still be treated as a secret.
Email contains the link, not the plaintext note.
AI summaries in an encrypted application
End-to-end encryption follows the below flow to support AI features.
The flow is:

• Decrypt the selected notes in the browser.

• Convert them into the text needed for the summary.

• Send that text ephemerally to the summarization endpoint.

• Return the generated summary to the client.
This keeps plaintext out of the database, but it is important to describe the boundary honestly: when a user requests an AI summary, the selected plaintext is temporarily processed by the application server and AI provider. It is not purely client-side inference.
For stronger privacy, a future version could support an on-device model or a user-selected local AI endpoint.
Search stays local
Cloud full-text search is incompatible with server-blind encrypted content unless you build a specialized searchable-encryption scheme, which comes with complexity and leakage trade-offs.
For this app, the simpler answer was the better one: search the decrypted local IndexedDB records.
The ⌘K / Ctrl+K interface queries the local note text and returns matching entries. It is fast, works offline, and does not require uploading a search index.
Authorization still matters when data is encrypted
Encryption does not replace access control.
Supabase Row Level Security policies still enforce who can insert, select, or update rows. For example, a share can only be created for a note owned by the authenticated user, and feedback must reference a valid share relationship.
This provides defense in depth:

• Encryption protects note content if the database is exposed.

• RLS prevents users from accessing rows they do not own.

• Rate limits reduce abuse of public and AI endpoints.

• A Content Security Policy helps reduce script-injection risk.
The last point is especially important. In browser-based E2EE, malicious JavaScript executing in the application origin can potentially access plaintext or locally stored key material. Preventing cross-site scripting, reviewing dependencies, and restricting script sources are part of the cryptographic threat model.
Features that had to change
Encryption affects product behavior beyond storage.
Automated server jobs cannot read notes. A daily cron job can send a generic notification or share metadata, but it cannot independently generate a plaintext summary from encrypted database content.
Account export also needs to happen from the client, where notes are available in decrypted form. The app supports JSON and Markdown downloads from the account panel.
These are not implementation details hidden from the product. They are consequences of deciding that the server should be blind.
What I learned
The most important lessons from this build were:
1. Local-first is a data model, not a caching strategy
Once IndexedDB becomes the source of truth, identity changes, merge rules, revisions, duplicate dates, and interrupted synchronization all need explicit behavior.
2. Encryption changes features
You cannot add E2EE as a database column and leave everything else untouched. Search, AI, email, sharing, exports, recovery, and background jobs all cross the plaintext boundary.
3. Recovery UX is part of security
Strong cryptography is not enough. Users need to understand why the service cannot reset their vault and why saving the recovery key matters.
4. Honest security language matters
“End-to-end encrypted” should not imply that every operation is invisible. Metadata remains on the server, AI summaries intentionally process selected plaintext, and anyone with a complete share URL can decrypt that share.
5. Start with the product habit
The architecture is interesting, but the app only succeeds if writing a daily note remains effortless. Security and synchronization should support that habit rather than bury it under setup.
Try it
You can try What I Did Today without creating an account. Write a daily note locally, organize work over time, and create an encrypted vault only when you want cloud synchronization.
The project began as a small solution to a personal productivity problem. Building it forced me to think deeply about data ownership, browser storage, key management, and what privacy means when an application also offers sharing and AI.
That tension made the product more complex, but it also made the final result more complete, refined, and rewarding to build.
